subsystem: ImageMagick MagickCore/coders 8BIM (Adobe Photoshop Image Resource Block) metadata parser. format8BIM() reads an 8BIM resource stream out of one Image blob (ifile), decodes each resource block's OSType marker, ID, Pascal-string name (PString) and variable-length data payload (str), and re-emits an ASCII '8BIM#ID#name=' textual representation into a second Image blob (ofile), with special handling for IPTC records. It is invoked when writing/converting the '8BIM'/'META' profile format.
provenance: Identified from ImageMagick MagickCore API surface: the Image* handle type, the ReadBlobByte / ReadBlobMSBSignedShort / ReadBlobMSBSignedLong / WriteBlobString blob accessors, the AcquireQuantumMemory / RelinquishMagickMemory allocator pair, FormatLocaleString, and the MagickPathExtent path/extent constant are all MagickCore identifiers. The helper symbols formatString, formatIPTCfromBuffer, THUMBNAIL_ID and IPTC_ID and the function name format8BIM come from ImageMagick's coders/meta.c. The in-file tag '/* sample: primevul_c_train_2540_vuln */' and the corpus filename train_0206_cwe772.c indicate a PrimeVul-sourced sample flagged for CWE-772 (missing resource release).
trust_boundary: ifile is an ImageMagick blob backed by an attacker-supplied image/profile file. Every value obtained via ReadBlobByte, ReadBlobMSBSignedShort and ReadBlobMSBSignedLong is fully attacker-controlled and may be crafted or truncated: the 4-byte OSType marker, the 16-bit ID, the 8-bit Pascal-string length plen, the raw PString bytes, the 32-bit MSB length count, and the count data bytes. The file can end (EOF) at any point, which is the primary adversarial lever here. ofile is a trusted output sink. The threat is a malicious 8BIM stream fed through image conversion (e.g. a web-facing convert/identify service).

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function takes an input blob ifile to parse and an output blob ofile to write the ASCII rendering into, returning an int (count of resources processed, or a negative error). temp[MagickPathExtent] is a fixed-size stack scratch buffer (MagickPathExtent is the ImageMagick path/extent constant, typically 4096) used to build the formatted output line. foundOSType is a flag (later voided out, so effectively dead). ID holds the 16-bit resource ID, resCount counts processed resources, i is a loop index, and c holds each byte returned by ReadBlobByte (declared int so it can hold EOF). count is a signed ssize_t byte count for the resource payload. PString and str are the two heap pointers this function allocates and must release — PString for the Pascal-string resource name, str for the variable-length data payload; these are the resources whose lifetime the CWE-772 label is about.

Initializes the resource counter to 0 and the (dead) foundOSType flag to 0, immediately casting it to void to suppress an unused-variable warning. It then primes the parse loop by reading the first byte of the blob into c and enters a while loop that continues until ReadBlobByte returns EOF. Each loop iteration is meant to locate and decode exactly one 8BIM resource block. Note that PString and str are NOT initialized to NULL here, so they hold indeterminate values until first assigned inside the loop body.

This is the OSType marker scan. When the current byte is ASCII '8', the code declares a 5-byte stack buffer, stores the '8' at index 0, then reads three more bytes into indices 1..3, bailing out with return(-1) on premature EOF. buffer[4]=0 NUL-terminates it so strcmp can compare the 4 characters against the literal "8BIM". The write range is indices 0..4 inclusive against a size-5 buffer, so this fixed buffer is correctly bounded. If the four bytes are not "8BIM", the code executes continue, which jumps back to the while(c != EOF) test WITHOUT reading a fresh byte — c still holds buffer[3]'s value, so the loop re-tests that stale byte.

If the current byte is not '8', the code simply reads the next byte and continues the scan, effectively skipping any byte that is not the start of an 8BIM marker. This is the fast-forward path that walks the blob until an '8' is seen.

Having confirmed the 8BIM OSType, the parser reads the 16-bit big-endian signed resource ID. A negative ID (which also covers the EOF/short-read case, since ReadBlobMSBSignedShort returns a negative sentinel on failure) triggers return(-1). At this point no heap memory has been allocated yet, so this early return leaks nothing.

This block decodes the Pascal-style resource name. It reads a single length byte into the 8-bit plen (0..255). It then heap-allocates PString with AcquireQuantumMemory sized (plen + MagickPathExtent) elements of 1 byte — deliberately over-allocated by a full MagickPathExtent (~4096) beyond the plen bytes it will store, so the subsequent fill and NUL are always in bounds. plen is promoted to int and added to the int constant MagickPathExtent, max 255+4096=4351, so the size computation cannot overflow. The loop reads plen bytes into PString[0..plen-1], returning -1 on EOF, then writes PString[plen]=0 to NUL-terminate. Finally, because 8BIM name fields are padded to an even total length, if plen is even (the length byte plus plen name bytes is odd, so one pad byte follows) it consumes one additional pad byte. From the AcquireQuantumMemory call at line 67 onward, PString is a live heap resource that every subsequent exit path must release.

This block reads the resource payload. count is a 32-bit big-endian signed value taken directly from the attacker's stream; a negative value returns -1. The code then allocates a str buffer of count bytes via AcquireQuantumMemory and fills it byte-by-byte, returning -1 on premature EOF. Because count is attacker-controlled up to 0x7FFFFFFF (~2 GiB) and is NOT bounded against the actual remaining file size, this is both a large-allocation lever and the point where a second live heap resource (str) is created alongside the already-live PString.

Unless the resource is a thumbnail (skipped by design), the parser renders the record. If the name is non-empty (strlen over the NUL-terminated PString) it formats '8BIM#<ID>#<name>=' into temp, otherwise '8BIM#<ID>='; FormatLocaleString is a bounded snprintf-style writer capped at MagickPathExtent, so temp cannot overflow even though PString can be up to 255 bytes. It writes temp to ofile, then emits the payload: for IPTC records it prefixes 'IPTC' and calls formatIPTCfromBuffer over the count payload bytes; otherwise it calls formatString over the same. Both consume exactly count bytes from str, which is correctly sized to count.

On the SUCCESSFUL path only, both heap buffers are freed via RelinquishMagickMemory (which returns NULL, reassigned to the pointers), the resource counter is incremented, and the next byte is read to drive the loop. When the loop finally sees EOF it returns resCount. The critical observation is that this pair of frees is the ONLY place PString and str are released — every early return inside the loop (there are several) bypasses it entirely.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This EOF check in the payload-fill loop returns -1 without freeing either str (allocated at line 91) or PString (allocated at line 67). Both live heap buffers are leaked. An attacker crafts an 8BIM record with a large count but truncates the file before the payload is fully delivered, forcing this path. This is the most damaging leak point because it drops TWO allocations per malicious record. The invariant the implementation must uphold: no return path between an AcquireQuantumMemory and the matching RelinquishMagickMemory may execute without first releasing every buffer already acquired (goto-cleanup or free-before-return).

Symmetric leak in the PString-fill loop: 'if (c == EOF) return -1;' abandons the PString buffer allocated at line 67. A file that ends partway through the Pascal-string bytes triggers it. Likewise the pad-byte read at line 84-85 ('if (c == EOF) return(-1);') and the count sanity check at line 89 ('if (count < 0) return -1;') and the str-allocation-failure branch at line 95 ('return 0;') all return while PString is live and none of them free it. Every one of these is a CWE-772 missing-release. Because format8BIM is called per profile during conversion, a service processing many hostile files accumulates these leaks into memory exhaustion (DoS).

count is a 32-bit attacker-controlled length checked only for negativity; a value up to 0x7FFFFFFF requests an ~2 GiB allocation that is never validated against the number of bytes actually remaining in the blob. A tiny input file declaring a huge count forces a giant allocation attempt (memory-pressure DoS), and if it succeeds the fill loop then spins reading EOF and leaks on the very first miss (the line 101 return). The invariant: a declared payload length must be bounded against the remaining input size before allocation, so a short file cannot demand an oversized buffer. Additionally count==0 yields AcquireQuantumMemory(0,1), whose ImageMagick contract can return NULL and drive the leaking 'return 0' at line 95.

When the four bytes after an '8' are not "8BIM", the else-branch does continue WITHOUT reading a new byte, so the while(c != EOF) test re-examines c, which still holds buffer[3]. If buffer[3] happens to be '8', the parser re-enters the '8' branch and reads three fresh bytes, resynchronizing one byte late; more importantly it can waste cycles re-testing an already-consumed byte. This is a parser-robustness/logic weakness rather than a memory-safety bug (no allocation is live at this point), but it means marker resynchronization is off-by-one relative to a clean 'read next byte then loop' design.

PString holds raw attacker bytes NUL-terminated only at index plen. Any embedded NUL truncates the strlen and the %s expansion, so the emitted name can silently differ from the on-disk bytes (metadata-integrity issue). This is NOT a buffer overflow: FormatLocaleString is length-capped at MagickPathExtent and PString is guaranteed NUL-terminated within its (plen+MagickPathExtent)-byte allocation, so the read stays in bounds. Worth confirming but low severity.

summary: A textbook CWE-772 case: format8BIM allocates PString (line 67) and str (line 91) with AcquireQuantumMemory but frees them only on the single success path (lines 127-128); every one of the several early error returns inside the parse loop (lines 77, 85, 89, 95, 101) leaks one or both buffers. A malicious or truncated 8BIM stream deterministically drives these paths, and the unbounded attacker-controlled count (line 88) additionally invites a ~2 GiB allocation — together enabling memory-exhaustion denial of service. No stack/heap overflow was found: buffer[5], PString, str and temp are each correctly sized for their writes.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Written only via FormatLocaleString with an explicit MagickPathExtent cap; bounded snprintf-style write, no overflow.
Dead flag: set then cast to void; never influences control flow or memory safety.
16-bit value, only used as %d in output and compared to THUMBNAIL_ID/IPTC_ID; negative rejected; no buffer contract.
Bounds are plen (<256) and count; index stays within the matching allocation, no OOB.
Correctly declared int to distinguish EOF from a 0xFF byte; each read is EOF-checked.
Attacker-controlled payload length; only negativity-checked, not bounded vs remaining input -> oversized allocation / leak driver.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: PString (unsigned char *, AcquireQuantumMemory line 67, free line 128)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Heap resource leaked on multiple early returns before the sole free at line 128 (CWE-772).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: str (unsigned char *, AcquireQuantumMemory line 91, free line 127)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Heap resource leaked on the payload-fill EOF return (line 101) and str-alloc-failure return (line 95) (CWE-772).
Writes indices 0..4 into a 5-byte buffer (three read bytes + '8' + trailing NUL); correctly bounded.
Max 255; buffer is over-allocated by plen+MagickPathExtent so PString[i] and PString[plen]=0 are always in bounds; size sum cannot overflow.
int promotion of plen (<=255) plus MagickPathExtent (~4096) = 4351 max; no integer overflow, and deliberate slack prevents write OOB.
plen<256 index into a buffer of at least MagickPathExtent bytes; in bounds.
Reads at most one pad byte; the EOF return here is folded into the PString leak finding.
The in-loop EOF return leaks both str and PString; core CWE-772 site.
PString is NUL-terminated within its allocation; read is in bounds. Embedded-NUL truncation is a metadata-fidelity nit, not memory safety.
Length-capped writer into temp; no overflow.
Consume exactly count bytes from a count-sized buffer; in bounds (unless callee mis-parses, out of scope for this file).
Uses raw printf instead of the MagickCore exception path and leaks PString at line 94; the leak is captured in the CWE-772 finding, the logging style is a minor code-quality nit.
continue without reading a fresh byte re-tests a stale c; parser-robustness weakness (low severity, no memory contract).
Correct on the success path but is the ONLY release site; its absence on every error path is the CWE-772 root cause.

findings.title: Heap memory leak of PString and str on every early error return (CWE-772)
findings.anchor: AcquireQuantumMemory for PString (line 67) and str (line 91) vs the sole RelinquishMagickMemory pair (lines 127-128); leaking returns at lines 77, 85, 89, 95, 101
findings.bug_class: Missing release of memory after effective lifetime (resource leak on error paths)
findings.invariant: Between AcquireQuantumMemory and the matching RelinquishMagickMemory, no control path (error return, allocation failure, EOF) may exit the function without releasing every buffer already acquired; use a single cleanup epilogue (goto fail) or free-before-return at each exit.
findings.failure_mode: PString is live from line 67 and str from line 91, yet both are freed only on the success path (lines 127-128). The str-fill EOF return at line 101 leaks BOTH buffers; the PString-fill EOF return at line 77, the pad-byte EOF return at line 85, the negative-count return at line 89, and the str-allocation-failure return at line 95 each leak PString. An attacker sends a truncated or malformed 8BIM record (e.g. a large declared count with the file ending mid-payload) to deterministically hit these paths. Repeated over many hostile inputs on a long-lived converter/service the leaks accumulate into memory exhaustion (denial of service).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that PString and str are NULL-initialized and that every return inside the while loop passes through a cleanup that calls RelinquishMagickMemory on both; check the upstream fixed version of coders/meta.c format8BIM for the added free-on-error paths.

findings.title: Unbounded attacker-controlled payload allocation from count (memory-pressure DoS)
findings.bug_class: Allocation of resources without limits / uncontrolled memory allocation
findings.invariant: A length prefix read from untrusted input must be validated against the number of bytes actually remaining in the blob before it is used as an allocation size, so a tiny file cannot demand a multi-gigabyte buffer.
findings.failure_mode: count is a 32-bit big-endian value taken straight from the stream and checked only for negativity, so it can be up to 0x7FFFFFFF (~2 GiB). A few-byte input declaring a huge count forces a giant allocation attempt (heavy memory pressure / possible OOM DoS); if the allocation fails, control takes the leaking return 0 at line 95, and if it succeeds the fill loop immediately hits EOF and leaks at line 101. count==0 additionally yields AcquireQuantumMemory(0,1), which per ImageMagick's contract may return NULL and again drive the leaking return.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether callers cap the 8BIM payload against the remaining blob length or a MagickResource limit, and whether ReadBlobMSBSignedLong is bounded by the profile size before this allocation.

findings.title: Off-by-one marker resynchronization on non-8BIM match
findings.invariant: After rejecting a partial marker, the scanner should advance to the next unconsumed byte before re-testing, so it neither re-processes an already-consumed byte nor skips input.
findings.failure_mode: When the four bytes after an '8' are not "8BIM", the else-branch does continue without reading a fresh byte, so the while test re-examines c (still buffer[3]). If that byte is '8', the parser re-enters the marker branch and reads three new bytes, resynchronizing one byte late; on adversarial input this only wastes cycles and mis-frames markers — no memory is live here so it is not a safety bug, but it is incorrect scanning.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm intended resync behavior against upstream and whether it can cause the parser to loop or miss legitimate markers on crafted input.

audit_checklist: Verify PString and str are NULL-initialized and that EVERY return inside the while loop (lines 39,57,64,77,85,89,95,101) releases both buffers before returning — this is the core CWE-772 fix.
Confirm the str allocation length count is bounded against the remaining blob/profile size before AcquireQuantumMemory (line 91), and handle count==0 without taking a leaking error path.
Check that ReadBlobMSBSignedShort/ReadBlobMSBSignedLong return a negative sentinel on short read so the ID<0 and count<0 guards actually catch truncation.
Confirm FormatLocaleString and formatString/formatIPTCfromBuffer treat PString/str as untrusted binary (embedded NULs, non-printables) and never over-read count/plen.
Re-examine the else/continue resync at lines 43-52 for correct byte advancement on non-8BIM data.
Replace raw printf("MemoryAllocationFailed") with the proper MagickCore exception/throw path and ensure it frees prior allocations.
